nativeRange.habitat: The Kerala Five Stripe Snakehead inhabits slow-moving hill streams, forested floodplain pools, and moist subtropical lowlands of the Western Ghats, in areas with dense riparian vegetation, submerged leaf litter, and seasonal water-level fluctuations. The trade name 'Kerala' refers to fish marketed from or associated with Kerala state in southwestern India, though some sources suggest collection near Alipurduar in West Bengal — meaning the precise provenance of trade stock remains unresolved.
nativeRange.biotope: Western Ghats hill stream / subtropical floodplain

decorPreferences: tight-fitting lid with humid air gap below
dim lighting with surface vegetation (Ceratopteris spp., floating plants)
dense submerged planting and leaf-litter cover
driftwood and rocks for territory delineation
minimal current — still or very slow flow

diet.notes: The Kerala Five Stripe Snakehead is an obligate piscivore and invertebrate predator in the wild. In captivity, most specimens can be transitioned to non-live alternatives including frozen and thawed fish flesh, earthworms, large bloodworms, krill, and shrimp. Juveniles are typically started on chironomid larvae (bloodworms) and gradually moved on to meatier items as they grow. Feeder goldfish should be avoided as a disease risk, and mammalian or avian meats are best left out of the diet as they have been linked to fatty liver disease in carnivorous fish. Feeding every two to three days is generally appropriate, with frequency reduced significantly during the cool winter period.

goodWith: robust large catfish (with extreme caution in very large tanks)
conspecific pairs possible only in large, heavily structured setups with close monitoring
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed
fin-nipping or fast-moving species that may stress the snakehead

summary: The Kerala Five Stripe Snakehead is an undescribed dwarf snakehead from the Western Ghats of India, reaching around 20 cm. Cool-tolerant and requiring seasonal temperature swings, it is an aggressive predator needing species-only housing, a secure lid, and experienced care. All snakeheads are federally prohibited in the United States.
careNotes:
The Kerala Five Stripe Snakehead is an undescribed species circulating in the specialist trade under the 'Kerala' locality label. Its molecular and morphological placement remains unresolved, with probable affinity to the C. gachua complex or the C. pulchra group. Adults reach approximately 20 cm, making this a mid-sized dwarf snakehead relative to the larger Channa species.

A species-only aquarium of at least 200 L with a footprint of 120 cm or longer is strongly recommended. The tank must have a tight-fitting, escape-proof lid — Channa are widely known to jump and can squeeze through surprisingly small gaps. A humid air gap of several centimetres should be left between the lid and the water surface, as these obligate air-breathers must have access to warm, moist air and can develop respiratory distress when exposed to cold drafts.

Unlike tropical snakeheads, this species is cool-tolerant and requires genuine seasonal temperature variation. The tank should be allowed to cool to 15–18°C in autumn and winter — unheated room temperature in a temperate climate is often sufficient — and then warm to 23–28°C through spring and summer. Feeding should be reduced to once a week or less during the cool phase. Fish kept at warm temperatures year-round may fail to thrive long-term, and breeding is unlikely without a proper cool period.

Water chemistry is broadly adaptable: pH 6.0–8.0 and soft to moderately hard water (gH 2–20) are acceptable. Weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are recommended. Filtration should provide gentle flow, as the species inhabits still and slow-moving waters in the wild.

Conspecific aggression tends to be high. Pairs can be kept in well-structured, large tanks but should be monitored closely. As an ambush predator, this snakehead will readily consume any tankmate small enough to fit in its mouth. Dense planting, driftwood, and rocks are important to break lines of sight and provide territory boundaries.

Hobbyists in the United States should be aware that the entire Channidae family (snakeheads) is listed as injurious wildlife under the Lacey Act (18 U.S.C. § 42). Import into the US and interstate transport are federally prohibited, regardless of any state-level possession rules.
breedingNotes: The Kerala Five Stripe Snakehead is a paternal mouthbrooder, meaning the male orally incubates the egg mass after spawning. Captive breeding has been achieved by European specialists, notably in Germany. A cool winter rest period of 15–18°C followed by a gradual spring warm-up appears to trigger spawning readiness. Females tend to be larger and deeper-bodied than males, and males may develop intensified blue-black colouration on the anal and dorsal fins during courtship. Spawning typically occurs near the surface among floating vegetation. The male broods both the eggs and the newly hatched fry; the young become free-swimming within one to two weeks and can accept small live foods such as micro-worms and baby brine shrimp once released from the male's care. The female should be removed from the tank if aggression increases after spawning.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Channa sp. 'five stripe'
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference. Provided tank size (≥120 cm), temperature range (seasonal 12–30°C basis), adult size (~20 cm), diet (piscivore adapting to dead foods), escape risk, mouthbrooder breeding, species-only recommendation, and surface air-breathing requirement. Also used fishy.wiki mirror data (temperature 15–28°C, pH 6.0–8.0, gH 2–20 dH, min tank 80 L) to fill numeric water-parameter gaps.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Channa sp. Kerala Five Stripe
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (~20 cm), species-only housing, tight lid requirement, seasonal temperature variation need, dim lighting and surface vegetation preferences, and paternal mouthbrooder breeding details. Page returned 403 at fetch time; data corroborated via search snippet and SeriouslyFish cross-reference.

additional.name: FishBase — Channa gachua (gachua complex reference)
additional.notes: Used as reference for C. gachua complex water-parameter baselines (temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–7.0, native South/Southeast Asian range) given probable affinity of Kerala Five Stripe to this complex. Kerala Five Stripe parameters broadened to reflect its cool-tolerant subtropical character.

additional.name: U.S. Geological Survey — Can aquarium hobbyists still possess snakeheads as pets?
additional.notes: Confirmed Lacey Act federal prohibition: importation and interstate transport of live snakeheads (entire Channidae family) is prohibited under U.S. federal law. Some states may permit intrastate possession but federal transport ban is absolute.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Channa sp. 5-stripe
additional.notes: German import/wholesaler record. Confirmed seasonal temperature range (12–30°C in nature), subtropical character, mouthbrooder reproductive mode, and maximum size of 15–20 cm. Listed origin as north India (Assam / North Bengal area) — consistent with unresolved trade provenance.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder bred by European specialists. A genuine cool winter rest at 15–18°C is required to trigger spawning readiness, followed by a gradual warm-up. Males incubate the egg mass orally; fry accept micro-worms and baby brine shrimp once free-swimming. The female should be separated if aggression escalates after spawning.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: All Channa species are USDA-listed Injurious Wildlife under the Lacey Act; import and interstate transport banned in the US. Undescribed Kerala (Western Ghats, India) species in trade as a small specialist snakehead.
